lines.text: The bus doors hiss open and spit you out into a river of people who all, somehow, already know where they're going.
Okay. Room 114. Room 114 is a place that exists. Findable. Probably.
You unfold the campus map. It's already soft at the creases from being refolded on the bus four times.
Cool, cool, love that for me. A map with no you-are-here dot.
...Great. Everyone else got the good map.
A torn bell schedule flutters against the wall. First bell in six minutes.
Six minutes to figure out who I'm going to be here. No pressure.
Locker 114 sticks like it's been rusted shut since before you were born.
Come on. Come ON—
It gives all at once, door swinging wide, binders detonating across the tile in a paper avalanche.
OH— and she's DOWN! Day one, folks, we already got a casualty!
A few kids nearby laugh — not cruel exactly, just loud, the way hallways are loud about everything.
...you okay?
lines.action: half-steps forward from the edge of the crowd

lines.text: She stops herself before she gets close enough for anyone to notice she offered.
Everyone's looking. Pick something. Say something. Do something.
Marcus tilts his head, reassessing you in real time.
Nah, you're good, you're good — that was funny. You're funny.
I have my moments. Mostly involving public humiliation, but hey. Moments.
Sit with us at lunch. I'm calling it. You're in.
That's a whole social tier unlocked in under ninety seconds. Weird flex, but okay.
He's already turning back to his friends, finger-gunning at you like he's stamping an entry pass.
...cool, cool. Love that for me.
Reyna crouches without a word, gathering the scattered binders into a neat stack, like she's done this before — for someone, if not for you.
You don't have to—
I know.
Locker's cursed. Ask anyone who's had it before you.
...Thanks. Seriously.
Reyna ducks her head — somewhere between a smile and an exit — and slips back into the crowd before you can say anything else.
She was gone before I even got her name. Great start, me.
The words come out before you can stop them.
What's so funny?
The hallway doesn't go silent, exactly — it just dips. Enough that you notice.
...whoa. Nobody said anything, man.
lines.emotion: eyebrow up, more curious than mad

lines.text: A couple of kids exchange a look and keep walking, already bored, already elsewhere.
Relax. It's a hallway, not a courtroom.
Great. First day, and I'm already the girl who yells at lockers.
...whatever.
The cafeteria hits you like a wall of noise and fluorescent light.
Marcus's table is loud, magnetic, laughing at something before it's even finished being said.
In the far corner, Reyna sits alone, pencil moving fast across a sketchbook, headphones half-on like a shield.
Everywhere else — just open seats. Nobody's decision but yours.
Okay. Tray's getting heavy. Pick a table, pick a life, apparently.
Okay, ranking the cafeteria pizza. Go. I need consensus before I commit.
Voices overlap — jokes, gossip, someone's story about a coach getting hit with a soccer ball, all folding over each other in easy rhythm.
Objectively a crime against bread. But I'll allow it.
See, this is why you're in.
Someone at the table nods toward the corner. 'Is that girl still drawing her weird monster stuff? Every single day.'
Reyna? Yeah, she's like a professional hermit. It's kind of her whole thing.
The table laughs. It's quick, it's small, it's gone in two seconds — and somehow that's what makes it land.
It's just a joke. It's not that deep. ...Is it?
Reyna looks up like she's bracing for the seat to be a joke.
...oh. Hey.
Mind if I sit? Everywhere else looked like it required a tribunal.
It's fine. I eat here most days. Nobody bothers you, you can actually hear yourself think.
She hesitates, then slides the sketchbook a few inches — not quite offering it, not quite hiding it either.
Okay, don't laugh, but I've been working on this since sixth grade. It's got, like, an actual plot now. Which is either really cool or a cry for help.
Can I see?
...Yeah. Okay. Yeah.
The drawings are dense, careful, strange in a good way — creatures with too many eyes and very specific expressions.
This is really good, Reyna.
It's kind of dumb, but— thanks.
lines.emotion: ducking her head, pleased despite herself

lines.text: You sit where nobody has to notice you, which is exactly the point.
From here you can see both worlds — Marcus's table erupting into laughter about something you'll never know the context for, and Reyna, alone, pencil never stopping.
Nobody's looking at me over here. That's the whole appeal.
...It's just really loud, being invisible.
The bell for next period feels far away. You eat. You watch. Nobody watches back.
Okay, okay, okay — settle down, we've got a partner project, due Friday. Partners, not permanent marriages, people. You can survive four days.
A ripple of movement — chairs scraping, friend groups already locking in.
Yo — c'mon, partner up, I got you.
Reyna glances over from her desk, quick, hopeful, and says nothing at all.
Devon, a few seats over, sketchbook already out, doesn't look up — just sits there, ready to be picked, like he's used to waiting.
Pick fast, people, or I'm assigning by birthday month, and nobody wants that chaos.
Three options. Three whole different weeks.
Marcus spins his chair around backwards and drops into it like the project's already handled.
So, weekend. You doing anything? There's this thing at Kayla's—
...We do have a project.
Right, right, the project. Yeah.
Okay, so — hear me out—
He nods toward a desk a few rows over, where Reyna's rough draft sits open and unattended while she's up sharpening a pencil.
She's not even using it right now. Thirty seconds. Nobody has to know.
That's not actually a small thing he just said.
Her handwriting is small, careful, precise — even her notes look like they were made by someone who cares.
A few lines practically write themselves into your outline before you can think too hard about it.
Yeah, that's good, put that in, whatever works.
It's fast. It's easy. It's also — something.
...yeah. Whatever works.
The knot in your stomach doesn't leave when you close the notebook.
We're not using her stuff. Let's just — come up with something.
Fine. Your funeral.
lines.emotion: already bored, already scrolling his phone

lines.text: Come on. You must have some idea. Anything.
...okay, what if the whole thing's set up like a hallway. Like, the story happens between classes. That's kind of funny, right?
...Actually? That's not bad.
I have ideas sometimes. It's rare. Treasure it.
It's thin. It's a little rushed. But it's actually yours.
You catch Reyna near the door before the bell, voice low.
Hey — just so you know, Marcus looked through your draft earlier. I didn't take anything, but... just be ready. Maybe.
Reyna goes still for a second, jaw tight.
...right. Okay.
Thanks for telling me.
She tucks the notebook closer to her chest, like she's already recalculating.
I'll have something ready. Don't worry about it.
I don't know if that helps her. But it felt like the least I could do.
...you want to hear my ideas? Like, actual ideas?
That's usually how projects work, yeah.
She hesitates, then flips her notebook open, angling it so you can actually see.
Okay, it's kind of dumb, but — what if we did it like a field guide? Like a monster field guide, except the monsters are just... normal school stuff. First days. Locker jams. That kind of thing.
Wait, that's actually really good.
Yeah?
Because then each 'monster' can be, like, a different anxiety, and we draw it—
lines.action: taps pen against sketchbook, thinking fast

lines.text: She's talking faster now, the careful sentences dropping away, replaced by something rambly and real.
I don't think people ask her for the whole idea very often.
So. Full disclosure. I already have, like, four ideas, and one of them involves a sentient locker. I'm not saying it's good. I'm saying it exists.
...Okay, I need to hear about the sentient locker.
It eats binders. That's the whole plot. It's a metaphor. For, uh, school.
lines.action: taps pencil against his knuckles, testing the joke

lines.text: That's either genius or deeply concerning.
Why not both.
He almost smiles — doesn't quite — and keeps sketching, the idea already taking shape on the page.
Okay. I'm in. Let's build the locker monster.
Knew you had taste.
Reyna stands at the front, notebook trembling slightly in her hands, presenting the field-guide idea to the class.
...so each entry catalogs a specific first-day anxiety, presented as a fictional creature—
...it's just weird, though? Like, monsters? For an English project?
A ripple of laughter moves through that side of the room.
Reyna's face goes still in a way that's worse than flinching.
Okay, continue, Reyna—
lines.action: still shuffling papers, hasn't caught it

lines.text: The room waits. Reyna doesn't move. Somebody has to.
Say something. Say something now.
Marcus presents your 'shared' idea first, breezing through it, casual, half-checked out.
...yeah, so it's kind of a field guide thing, monsters, whatever, it's got some solid lines in it—
Then Reyna gets up. And her project is the same idea. The same lines, even.
...wait.
She looks up, scanning the room, landing on you.
...huh. Okay, let's — hold on a second.
...that's my line. That's — that's literally my line.
There it is. The thing you knew was coming.
It's not weird. It's actually really smart — way smarter than most of what's in this room, honestly.
The room goes quiet for exactly one beat too long.
Then a couple of kids actually nod, murmuring agreement, and the moment tips.
...I mean, I didn't say it was bad, I just said—
lines.emotion: caught off guard, half-laughing it off

lines.text: You said it loud enough for the whole room, though.
Marcus doesn't have a comeback for that. He shrugs instead, looking almost impressed despite himself.
...you didn't have to do that. Back there.
I know.
I'm just saying. You didn't have to.
Your throat goes tight. The words are right there. They don't come out.
Reyna sits back down, smaller than she was a minute ago, notebook pulled tight against her chest.
Okay, wait, whose turn is it—
lines.emotion: already onto something else, laughing with his table

lines.text: It's not my fight. It's not my fight. It's not—
Ms. Alvarez calls the next group up. Class moves on like nothing happened.
Nobody noticed I didn't say anything. That doesn't make it feel any better.
Okay, but has anyone here actually fought a hallway monster before eight a.m.? Because I have, and it was way scarier than this project.
A few laughs — different laughs this time, the kind that break tension instead of building it.
The class's attention slides sideways, off Reyna, onto you, just long enough for her to sit back down without an audience.
...yeah, okay, fair.
lines.emotion: blinking, already forgetting he started it

lines.text: Later, passing her desk, you lean down, quiet.
For what it's worth — I thought it was really good.
...thanks.
Marcus never quite clocks that he lost that round. Reyna does.
You start to explain, and the excuse dies somewhere in your throat.
...Actually — can I just say something?
The best lines in ours were hers. I used her notes without asking. That's on me.
...whatever. It's not a big deal.
lines.emotion: quieter now, losing the performance voice

lines.text: A few classmates murmur — not mocking, this time. Something closer to respect.
Okay — let's talk about that after class, yeah? Good. Moving on.
That felt awful. It also felt like the only thing to do.
You say nothing. Marcus recovers first.
I mean — great minds, right? Weird coincidence.
A few kids laugh along. It smooths right over, the way these things do when nobody pushes back.
Reyna doesn't argue. She just sits down and doesn't look up again.
The bell rings eventually. The guilt doesn't.
After the bell, you catch Ms. Alvarez before she erases the board.
Hey — um, that idea Marcus and I presented? It was actually Reyna's, mostly. I just wanted you to know.
...Thank you for telling me. That took something.
lines.action: studies you a second, nodding slowly

lines.text: The next day, she makes a point of holding up Reyna's original draft in front of the class, praising it specifically.
Reyna never quite finds out why. But she sits a little straighter after that.
Not everything gets fixed out loud. Some things just get fixed.
The sign-up sheet for the fall talent show hangs crooked on the activities board, half the lines already filled with familiar names.
Marcus's crowd has claimed most of the top slots — dance crew, 'best group video,' the safe, loud stuff.
Reyna lingers a few feet away, pen uncapped, not quite committing to the page.
...it's dumb. Nobody signs up for art displays.
Somebody's gotta be first.
...yeah. Maybe.
She looks back at the sheet. So do you.
One line. My name. My actual thing, not the impressive-sounding version.
You write it down before you can talk yourself out of it — the thing you actually love, not the one that sounds good out loud.
Reyna watches you do it. Then, slower, she picks up the pen.
...okay.
She signs her name under Art Showcase in small, careful letters, like she's trying not to take up too much space even on paper.
That's not dumb, by the way. For the record.
...yeah, well. Neither's yours.
Neither of you says anything else. You don't have to.
You look at your real answer for a second — then write down the thing Marcus's crowd already claimed instead.
There she is. Knew you'd come around.
lines.action: claps you on the back, grinning

lines.text: ...Yeah. Should be fun.
It feels like winning. Mostly.
Mostly.
You read the sheet twice. Your pen hovers over an empty line.
If I don't sign anything, nobody has to see me try. Nobody has to see me fail, either.
You cap the pen and walk away, the sheet still blank where your name could've gone.
The hallway empties out slow around you, lockers clicking shut one by one.
Safer. Definitely safer.
The bus stop is nearly empty, late afternoon light stretched thin across the sidewalk.
...so. Same bus tomorrow?
Same bus tomorrow.
Cool. I mean — okay. Good.
It wasn't the easiest first day. It still felt, somehow, like the beginning of something actually yours.
Your phone buzzes before you're even off school property.
A meme from Marcus. Then another.
...heh.
lines.emotion: small laugh, alone at the bus stop

lines.text: The bus stop is empty. The laugh doesn't quite fill it.
Today went well. I think. Mostly.
The bus pulls up right on time. Nobody saves you a seat, but nobody's saving one against you, either.
Survived. Technically.
You slide into a window seat and watch Ridgeview shrink behind the glass.
Nobody laughed at me today. Nobody really saw me either.

options.text: Well, that happened. Ten out of ten locker, would not recommend.
Just grab everything and go. Fast.
What's so funny?

a.text: Wait— okay, that was actually kind of funny.
I contain multitudes. Mostly folders, apparently.

b.text: You duck down, stacking papers as fast as your hands will move, eyes on the floor.

c.text: The words come out sharper than you meant them to.
...whoa. Okay. Didn't say anything, did I?

options.text: Sit with Marcus. Worse decisions have been made.
Head for the corner table. Quieter's fine.
Just find an empty table. Watch first.

a.text: There she is! Come on, sit, sit—

b.text: You can sit. If you want.

c.text: You pick a table with nobody at it and put your tray down like it's a decision that doesn't count.

options.text: Partner with Marcus.
Partner with Reyna.
Partner with Devon.

a.text: That's what I'm talking about. We're gonna be done in like ten minutes, easy.

options.text: Grab it. Quick, before she's back.
No. We're doing our own thing.
Skim it fast — and tell her later.

a.text: See, this is why I picked you.

c.text: You flip through her notes fast, quiet, heart going a little too hard for something you're telling yourself is fine.

options.text: It's not weird. It's good — say it.
Say nothing. Let it pass.
Crack a joke to change the subject, then tell her later.

a.text: The room holds its breath for a second, waiting to see if you mean it.

b.text: The silence stretches a beat too long before class just... moves on.

c.text: You feel the room's attention shift, just slightly, off Reyna and onto you.

options.text: Tell the class the idea was hers.
Stay quiet. Let Marcus handle it.
Say nothing now — tell Ms. Alvarez after class.

a.text: The sentence is out before you can second-guess it. There's no taking it back now.

b.text: I mean — great minds, right? Weird coincidence.

c.text: You let the moment pass in the room — but you're already rehearsing what you'll say after the bell.

options.text: Sign up for the thing I actually love.
Sign up for what Marcus's crowd is doing.
Don't sign anything. Just walk.

a.text: The pen feels heavier than it should for something this small.

b.text: It's the easier name to write. That's kind of the whole problem.

c.text: You keep walking. The sheet stays exactly the same behind you.

lines.text: You know, when you sat down at my table today, I figured it was, like, a one-time thing. Pity lunch.
Wasn't pity. Bad food's a great equalizer, though.
...I still can't believe you actually put your name on that sheet. In pen. No takebacks.
Yeah, well. You did too.
Guess we're both stuck now.
yo you're officially in the group chat. welcome to the inner circle lol
Inner circle. Day one. That's supposed to feel like winning.
I keep thinking about the corner table. The one I didn't sit at.
...Whatever. Tomorrow's a whole different bus.
The sign-up sheet's probably still hanging there. Blank where my name could've gone.
I keep telling myself there's always tomorrow. I don't fully believe it yet.
Still — nobody laughed. Nobody pointed. That has to count for something. Doesn't it?

voiceNotes: Jordan (player) deflects hard with humor when nervous, and her internal lines run faster/more clipped in tense moments, slower and plainer when she means something ('I know.' / 'Same bus tomorrow.'). Marcus's dialogue is almost entirely performance ('bro,' hype-man cadence, finger-guns) until called out directly, at which point his lines shrink to short, deflated fragments ('...whatever. It's not a big deal.') — that collapse IS his honesty. Reyna starts clipped and exit-oriented ('I know.' / short sentences) and only opens into rambly, specific enthusiasm once she trusts the player (the field-guide pitch), which should read as a small trust event, not just character quirk. Devon's humor lands because he never laughs at his own jokes — the deadpan is the joke. Ms. Alvarez's warmth and overextension show in her multitasking speech (claps, 'okay okay okay,' pen-tapping while triaging) and in the fact she almost misses the cruelty in scene-8 — that's a deliberate flaw, not just background color.

alternatives: That locker's the worst one. For what it's worth.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Keeps Reyna's hesitant, minimal-words instinct while offering slightly different levels of warmth depending on desired subtlety.

alternatives: It's not weird. It's better than half the stuff people actually clapped for.
Maybe try actually listening before you laugh next time.
alternativeLines.reasoning: First alternative is a bit softer/less confrontational if a gentler 'stood up' tone is preferred; second is sharper if the scene should feel more pointed.
The line needs to sound like resignation dressed as ease — these alternatives lean more openly hollow if the branch should read less ambiguous.
